title: Johnson & Johnson — Immunology — Knowledge Doc
html:
Market Context: TREMFYA (guselkumab) in IL-23-Mediated Immune Diseases

Product: TREMFYA (guselkumab) Manufacturer: Johnson & Johnson Innovative Medicine (Janssen Biotech) Indication: Plaque psoriasis, psoriatic arthritis, ulcerative colitis, Crohn's disease — spanning Dermatology, Gastroenterology, and Rheumatology Document scope: Product-level competitive/clinical landscape (not wave-specific) Last updated: April 2026

1. Disease Landscape

1.1 Epidemiology

TREMFYA operates across three distinct sub-TAs unified by the IL-23 pathway:

Plaque Psoriasis:

  • ~8 million Americans affected; ~3 million with moderate-to-severe disease requiring systemic therapy
  • Chronic, immune-mediated skin disease with visible plaques causing significant quality-of-life impact
  • Biologic-eligible population: ~500,000-800,000 patients in the US

Psoriatic Arthritis (PsA):

  • Affects ~30% of psoriasis patients (~1-2 million in the US)
  • Inflammatory arthritis with joint damage, enthesitis, dactylitis, and axial involvement
  • Overlap with psoriasis creates dual-domain treatment decisions (skin + joints)

Ulcerative Colitis (UC):

  • ~1 million US patients; ~300,000-400,000 with moderate-to-severe disease
  • Chronic inflammatory bowel disease confined to the colon; relapsing-remitting course
  • High unmet need in biologic-experienced patients (JAK inhibitor and anti-TNF failures)

Crohn's Disease (CD):

  • ~800,000 US patients; ~250,000-350,000 with moderate-to-severe disease
  • Transmural inflammation anywhere in the GI tract; more heterogeneous than UC
  • High surgical burden (~50% require surgery within 10 years of diagnosis)

1.2 IL-23 Biology

Interleukin-23 (IL-23) is a heterodimeric cytokine (p19 + p40 subunits) that plays a central role in driving pathogenic Th17 cell differentiation and maintenance. IL-23 sits upstream of IL-17 in the inflammatory cascade:

  • IL-23 → Th17 cells → IL-17A/IL-17F → tissue inflammation (skin, joints, gut)
  • Selective IL-23 inhibitors (targeting p19 subunit) block this cascade at the source without broadly suppressing IL-12 (which shares the p40 subunit and is important for host defense)
  • This selectivity explains the favorable safety profile of IL-23 inhibitors vs. broader immunosuppressants

1.3 Standard Treatment Algorithms

Plaque Psoriasis:

Mild: Topicals (corticosteroids, vitamin D analogues, calcineurin inhibitors)
  |
Moderate-to-severe: Phototherapy → Systemic therapy
  |
  ├── Traditional oral systemics: methotrexate, cyclosporine, acitretin, apremilast
  ├── Biologics:
  |     ├── Anti-TNF (adalimumab, etanercept, infliximab, certolizumab)
  |     ├── Anti-IL-17 (secukinumab/Cosentyx, ixekizumab/Taltz, bimekizumab/Bimzelx)
  |     ├── Anti-IL-23 (TREMFYA, Skyrizi/risankizumab, Stelara/ustekinumab)
  |     └── Anti-IL-36 (spesolimab — for generalized pustular psoriasis)
  └── **Oral IL-23: ICOTYDE (icotrokinra) — newly approved March 2026**

Ulcerative Colitis / Crohn's Disease:

Mild-moderate: 5-ASA (mesalamine), budesonide, corticosteroids
  |
Moderate-to-severe: Immunomodulators (azathioprine, 6-MP, methotrexate)
  |
  └── Biologics / advanced therapies:
        ├── Anti-TNF (infliximab, adalimumab, vedolizumab — anti-integrin)
        ├── Anti-IL-12/23 (Stelara/ustekinumab)
        ├── Anti-IL-23 (TREMFYA, Skyrizi — both recently approved/expanding)
        ├── JAK inhibitors (tofacitinib, upadacitinib — UC; upadacitinib — CD)
        └── S1P modulators (ozanimod — UC)

1.4 Unmet Needs

  1. Oral biologics for psoriasis: Until ICOTYDE, all IL-23-targeting therapies required injection. Oral convenience could reshape the treatment paradigm — and ICOTYDE is J&J's own product
  2. Durable remission in IBD: Many patients cycle through multiple biologics; sustained steroid-free remission without immunosuppression is the goal
  3. Biologic-experienced GI patients: Patients who fail anti-TNF + vedolizumab + JAK inhibitors have limited options; IL-23 inhibitors are emerging as effective in this refractory population
  4. Psoriatic arthritis with incomplete skin response: Patients with both skin and joint disease need a single agent that addresses both domains effectively
  5. Head-to-head data in GI: Most GI biologic approvals are placebo-controlled; physicians want comparative effectiveness data to guide sequencing
  6. Safety in long-term immunosuppression: Patients with chronic immune diseases are on therapy for decades; long-term infection, malignancy, and cardiovascular safety data is critical

2. Product Profiles

2.1 TREMFYA (guselkumab) — Johnson & Johnson

Mechanism of Action: TREMFYA is a human IgG1 lambda monoclonal antibody that selectively binds the p19 subunit of IL-23, blocking its interaction with the IL-23 receptor. By targeting IL-23 specifically (rather than IL-12/IL-23 shared p40 subunit like ustekinumab), TREMFYA selectively inhibits the Th17 pathway while preserving IL-12-mediated Th1 immune surveillance.

Approved Indications:

  • Plaque psoriasis: Moderate-to-severe in adults (approved 2017)
  • Psoriatic arthritis: Active PsA in adults (approved 2020)
  • Ulcerative colitis: Moderate-to-severe in adults (approved 2025)
  • Crohn's disease: Moderate-to-severe in adults (approved 2025)

Key Efficacy Data:

Psoriasis (VOYAGE 1 & 2):

  • PASI 90 at week 48: ~80-84% (TREMFYA) vs. ~53% (adalimumab) — demonstrated superiority over anti-TNF
  • PASI 100 (complete clearance): ~44-50% at week 48
  • Durable response with dosing every 8 weeks after induction

Psoriatic Arthritis (DISCOVER-1 & 2):

  • ACR20 at week 24: ~59-64% vs. ~33% (placebo)
  • Simultaneous skin improvement (PASI 90: ~45-50%)
  • Inhibition of structural joint damage on radiographs

Ulcerative Colitis (QUASAR):

  • Clinical remission at week 12 (induction): ~24% vs. ~8% (placebo)
  • Clinical remission at week 44 (maintenance): ~49% vs. ~26% (placebo)
  • Endoscopic improvement and histologic remission endpoints also met

Crohn's Disease (GALAXI):

  • Clinical remission (CDAI <150) at week 48: ~50-58% (dose-dependent) vs. ~19% (placebo)
  • Endoscopic response: ~42-48% vs. ~13%
  • Demonstrated efficacy in both bio-naive and bio-experienced patients

Safety Profile:

  • Generally well-tolerated with a favorable long-term safety profile
  • Most common AEs: upper respiratory infections, headache, injection site reactions, arthralgia, diarrhea, gastroenteritis
  • Serious infections: Low rate (~1-2%), comparable to other IL-23 inhibitors
  • No significant signal for malignancy, MACE, or opportunistic infections in pooled analyses
  • No boxed warnings — a differentiator vs. JAK inhibitors (which carry boxed warnings for MACE, malignancy, thrombosis)

Dosing/Administration:

  • Psoriasis/PsA: 100 mg SubQ at weeks 0, 4, then every 8 weeks — convenient Q8W maintenance
  • UC induction: IV infusion at weeks 0, 4, 8; then SubQ 100 mg Q8W or Q4W maintenance
  • CD induction: IV infusion; then SubQ maintenance (similar to UC)
  • Self-administered SubQ injections at home (maintenance phase); pre-filled syringe or autoinjector

Access/Reimbursement:

  • Established payer coverage across all approved indications
  • Specialty pharmacy distribution
  • Patient support programs (Janssen CarePath)

2.2 Skyrizi (risankizumab-rzaa) — AbbVie

Mechanism of Action: Selective anti-IL-23 p19 antibody — same target as TREMFYA. Humanized IgG1 monoclonal antibody.

Approved Indications: Plaque psoriasis, psoriatic arthritis, Crohn's disease, ulcerative colitis — mirrors TREMFYA's indication portfolio.

Key Efficacy Data:

  • Psoriasis (UltIMMa-1/2): PASI 90 at week 52: ~82-87%; PASI 100: ~56-60% — numerically higher than TREMFYA in cross-trial comparisons
  • Crohn's Disease (ADVANCE/MOTIVATE/FORTIFY): Clinical remission at week 52: ~52% vs. ~41% (ustekinumab, head-to-head in SEQUENCE trial) — the only head-to-head win in CD for an IL-23 vs. another biologic
  • Ulcerative Colitis (INSPIRE): Clinical remission ~40% at induction; maintenance data strong
  • PsA (KEEPsAKE-1/2): ACR20 ~57% at week 24

Safety Profile:

  • Similar to TREMFYA — favorable long-term safety; no boxed warnings
  • Upper respiratory infections, headache, fatigue, injection site reactions

Market Position:

  • AbbVie's flagship immunology asset post-Humira biosimilar erosion
  • Rapidly growing revenue (~$10B+ annualized run rate)
  • Aggressive commercial investment; large sales force
  • Slightly outperforming TREMFYA on SDP effectiveness in GI per tracking data — a competitive signal to watch
  • SEQUENCE trial (Skyrizi vs. Stelara head-to-head in Crohn's) is a powerful promotional asset — TREMFYA lacks a comparable head-to-head win in GI

2.3 Stelara (ustekinumab) — Johnson & Johnson

Mechanism of Action: Anti-IL-12/23 antibody targeting the shared p40 subunit. Blocks both IL-12 (Th1) and IL-23 (Th17) pathways — less selective than TREMFYA or Skyrizi.

Market Position:

  • Biosimilar erosion accelerating — multiple biosimilars launched in 2024-2025
  • Revenue declining rapidly; no longer a growth driver
  • Still prescribed by physicians with long experience, but new starts shifting to IL-23 p19 agents
  • Important context: TREMFYA is J&J's successor to Stelara in the IL-23 space

2.4 Cosentyx (secukinumab) — Novartis

Mechanism of Action: Anti-IL-17A monoclonal antibody. Blocks IL-17A downstream of IL-23, directly inhibiting the effector cytokine rather than the upstream driver.

Relevance:

  • Strong in psoriasis and PsA; faster onset of action than IL-23 inhibitors in skin
  • Not approved for IBD — IL-17 blockade can worsen IBD (a key differentiator favoring IL-23 inhibitors in patients with GI comorbidity)
  • Competes with TREMFYA primarily in derm and rheum, not gastro

2.5 Bimzelx (bimekizumab) — UCB

Mechanism of Action: Dual IL-17A and IL-17F inhibitor — the broadest IL-17 blockade available.

Key Efficacy Data:

  • Highest PASI 100 rates in psoriasis (~60-70%) — potentially best-in-class for complete skin clearance
  • Strong PsA data; approved in multiple indications

Relevance:

  • Competes on skin clearance superiority in psoriasis
  • Same IBD limitation as Cosentyx (IL-17 blockade contraindicated in IBD)
  • Growing market presence in derm

2.6 ICOTYDE (icotrokinra) — Johnson & Johnson

Mechanism of Action: First-in-class oral cyclic peptide that selectively antagonizes the IL-23 receptor. Unlike injectable IL-23 antibodies (TREMFYA, Skyrizi), ICOTYDE is an oral tablet — a fundamentally different modality delivering the same pathway blockade.

Key Efficacy Data:

  • PASI 90 and PASI 100 rates comparable to injectable IL-23 inhibitors in pivotal trials
  • Approved March 2026 for moderate-to-severe plaque psoriasis (adults and pediatric patients >=12 years, >=40 kg)

Market Position:

  • J&J's own oral complement to TREMFYA — not a competitor but a portfolio expansion
  • $5B+ peak revenue projected by J&J
  • Oral convenience could reshape the psoriasis treatment algorithm: patients who resist injectables now have a targeted oral IL-23 option
  • May cannibalize some TREMFYA psoriasis share, but J&J retains the revenue within the portfolio
  • Does NOT currently have GI or PsA indications — TREMFYA retains multi-TA breadth advantage

Relevance for Tracking:

  • ICOTYDE PET is flagged as CRITICAL in J&J's planning documents
  • The oral vs. injectable IL-23 positioning will be a key promotional narrative
  • Cross-brand messaging between TREMFYA (injectable, multi-indication) and ICOTYDE (oral, psoriasis) needs tracking

3. Competitive Dynamics

3.1 Head-to-Head Positioning (IL-23 Inhibitors)

Attribute TREMFYA Skyrizi Stelara ICOTYDE
Target IL-23 p19 IL-23 p19 IL-12/23 p40 IL-23 receptor (oral)
Administration SubQ Q8W SubQ Q12W (psoriasis) SubQ Q12W Oral daily
Psoriasis PASI 90 ~80-84% ~82-87% ~55-60% Comparable to injectables
Crohn's remission ~50-58% ~52% ~40-45% Not approved
UC remission ~49% (maintenance) ~40% ~44% Not approved
PsA ACR20 ~59-64% ~57% ~55% Not approved
Head-to-head in GI None SEQUENCE (beat Stelara in CD) Comparator in SEQUENCE N/A
Dosing frequency Q8W (SubQ) Q12W (psoriasis), Q8W (GI) Q12W Daily oral
Boxed warning None None None TBD
Biosimilar risk Years away Years away Active erosion N/A (small molecule peptide)
Multi-TA coverage Psoriasis + PsA + UC + CD Psoriasis + PsA + UC + CD Psoriasis + PsA + UC + CD Psoriasis only (currently)

3.2 Head-to-Head Positioning by Sub-TA

Dermatology (Psoriasis):

Agent Class PASI 100 Key Advantage
Bimzelx IL-17A/F ~60-70% Highest skin clearance
Skyrizi IL-23 p19 ~56-60% Strong clearance + durability
TREMFYA IL-23 p19 ~44-50% Proven durability; Q8W convenience
ICOTYDE Oral IL-23R Comparable Oral convenience
Cosentyx IL-17A ~30-40% Fast onset

Gastroenterology (UC + CD):

Agent Class Key Advantage
Skyrizi IL-23 p19 SEQUENCE head-to-head data in CD
TREMFYA IL-23 p19 Broadest approved GI portfolio (UC + CD)
Stelara IL-12/23 p40 Long track record; biosimilar pricing
Upadacitinib JAK inhibitor Oral; rapid onset; but boxed warning

3.3 Guideline Status

AAD/NPF Guidelines (Psoriasis):

  • IL-23 inhibitors (TREMFYA, Skyrizi) and IL-17 inhibitors (Cosentyx, Taltz, Bimzelx): First-line biologic options
  • ICOTYDE: Expected to receive guideline inclusion as first targeted oral option

ACG/AGA Guidelines (IBD):

  • Anti-TNF agents remain first-line biologic in many guidelines
  • IL-23 inhibitors (TREMFYA, Skyrizi) gaining positioning as preferred second-line or alternatives to anti-TNF
  • JAK inhibitors listed but with safety caveats (boxed warnings)

3.4 Key Competitive Differentiators for TREMFYA

Strengths:

  1. Broadest approved IL-23 portfolio: Psoriasis + PsA + UC + CD — covers all three sub-TAs
  2. Q8W maintenance dosing: Convenient injection schedule for patients
  3. Strong safety profile: No boxed warnings; favorable long-term data across indications
  4. J&J portfolio synergy: Same sales force relationships as ICOTYDE; cross-referral potential
  5. MSL engagement leadership: J&J MSLs dominate GI engagement with highest SOV and reach vs. competitors

Vulnerabilities:

  1. Skyrizi's SEQUENCE head-to-head data: Skyrizi beat Stelara in Crohn's — TREMFYA has no comparable head-to-head GI win. Physicians cite this as a differentiator
  2. Skyrizi slightly outperforming on SDP effectiveness in GI (per tracking data) — despite TREMFYA's SOV leadership
  3. ICOTYDE may cannibalize psoriasis share: Oral convenience is powerful; some TREMFYA psoriasis patients may switch
  4. Dosing frequency vs. Skyrizi: In psoriasis, Skyrizi is Q12W vs. TREMFYA Q8W — a convenience advantage for Skyrizi
  5. AbbVie's commercial muscle: Skyrizi has massive promotional investment post-Humira; AbbVie's immunology sales force is the largest in the industry

4. Market Context

4.1 Market Size and Segmentation

  • Psoriasis biologic market (US): ~$15-18B annually; growing with new entrants
  • IBD biologic/advanced therapy market (US): ~$12-15B annually; fastest-growing segment
  • PsA biologic market: ~$5-7B; overlaps significantly with psoriasis market
  • Total IL-23 addressable market: $30B+ across all indications

4.2 Target Prescriber Universe

  • Dermatologists: ~12,000 in the US; ~3,000-5,000 actively prescribe biologics for psoriasis
  • Gastroenterologists: ~15,000 in the US; ~5,000-7,000 manage moderate-to-severe IBD
  • Rheumatologists: ~5,000-6,000 in the US; many manage PsA alongside dermatologists
  • Key dynamic: TREMFYA spans three specialist audiences with distinct information needs, competitive sets, and prescribing behaviors. The same brand must be messaged differently to a dermatologist (skin clearance focus), gastroenterologist (remission + endoscopic healing focus), and rheumatologist (joint + skin dual-domain focus)

4.3 Key Market Dynamics

  1. IL-23 class is winning the biologic war: IL-23 inhibitors are displacing anti-TNF and IL-17 agents as preferred biologics across psoriasis and IBD due to superior efficacy-safety balance
  2. Oral disruption is here: ICOTYDE's approval reshapes psoriasis — patients who refused injectables now have a targeted oral option. The injectable-to-oral shift in psoriasis mirrors what happened in rheumatology with JAK inhibitors
  3. Skyrizi vs. TREMFYA is the central GI rivalry: Both are IL-23 p19 agents with similar mechanisms. Differentiation is on data (SEQUENCE favors Skyrizi), dosing (Q12W vs. Q8W in psoriasis), and commercial execution
  4. Stelara biosimilar pricing pressure: As Stelara biosimilars become widely available, the IL-12/23 option becomes cheaper — creating a cost-based competitor that IL-23 p19 agents must outperform on efficacy to justify premium pricing
  5. JAK inhibitor safety concerns benefit IL-23 class: Boxed warnings on JAK inhibitors (upadacitinib, tofacitinib) create a safety halo for IL-23 inhibitors in GI; physicians increasingly prefer IL-23 over JAK for safety-conscious patients

4.4 Prescribing Decision Drivers

  1. Efficacy (skin clearance in derm; remission in GI): Physicians want the deepest, most durable response
  2. Safety profile: Long-term safety data and absence of boxed warnings are strong differentiators
  3. Administration convenience: Oral (ICOTYDE) > SubQ self-injection > IV infusion; injection frequency matters (Q12W > Q8W > Q4W)
  4. Multi-domain coverage: For PsA patients with both skin and joint disease, agents that address both are preferred
  5. Prior biologic experience: Sequencing after anti-TNF failure is the key GI decision; IL-23 data in bio-experienced patients is critical
  6. Cost/payer access: Step therapy requirements, prior authorization, and copay support influence first-line biologic choice

5. Pipeline and Future Expansion

5.1 TREMFYA Pipeline

Program Indication Phase Status
TREMFYA in GI (label expansion) Additional GI indications, combination studies Various Ongoing
TREMFYA Global Proposal SFE, MEE, Rx Integration across markets Commercial $568K proposal submitted March 2026
TREMFYA IMM PET Takeover Full immunology PET ($250-350K) BD 2026 planning underway — Rank 1 BD opportunity

5.2 ICOTYDE Pipeline

Program Indication Phase Status
Plaque psoriasis (approved) Moderate-to-severe Approved March 2026 — active launch
Psoriatic arthritis Active PsA Phase 3 (likely) Expected
IBD (UC, CD) Moderate-to-severe TBD Would be transformative if oral IL-23 works in GI

5.3 Competitor Pipeline

Agent Company Development Expected Impact
Skyrizi label expansions AbbVie Broader IBD, PsA combinations Deepens the TREMFYA rivalry
Bimzelx in IBD UCB Phase 3 If IL-17A/F works in GI (despite historical concerns), would disrupt IL-23 class positioning
Mirikizumab (Omvoh) Eli Lilly Approved for UC Another IL-23 p19 agent; growing presence in GI
TL1A inhibitors Multiple (Roche, Merck, others) Phase 2-3 Novel GI mechanism targeting fibrosis + inflammation; could reshape IBD treatment

6. Key Themes for Tracking

6.1 Primary Promotional Narratives

Brand Core Message Supporting Data
TREMFYA "Broadest IL-23 portfolio across derm, gastro, and rheum" Only IL-23 p19 approved in psoriasis + PsA + UC + CD
Skyrizi "Best-in-class IL-23 with head-to-head data" SEQUENCE (beat Stelara in CD); Q12W dosing in psoriasis
ICOTYDE "First targeted oral IL-23 for psoriasis" Oral convenience; comparable efficacy to injectables
Bimzelx "Highest skin clearance in psoriasis" PASI 100 ~60-70%

6.2 Anticipated Competitive Battlegrounds

  1. TREMFYA vs. Skyrizi in GI: The defining rivalry. Skyrizi has SEQUENCE data; TREMFYA must differentiate on portfolio breadth, safety profile depth, and MSL engagement quality. Track physician perception of comparative effectiveness
  2. ICOTYDE launch trajectory in psoriasis: How fast does oral IL-23 displace injectable IL-23 and IL-17 agents? Track dermatologist awareness, trial, and switching behavior
  3. Injectable-to-oral conversion in psoriasis: Monitor whether TREMFYA psoriasis patients switch to ICOTYDE, and whether J&J positions this as portfolio migration or holds the line
  4. MSL effectiveness in GI: J&J MSLs lead on SOV but Skyrizi slightly outperforms on SDP effectiveness per tracking — closing this gap is critical
  5. Stelara biosimilar impact: As Stelara becomes cheaper, do GI physicians "trade down" or stay on p19 agents? Track biologic switching patterns
  6. TL1A inhibitors as emerging GI disruptors: Watch for awareness and interest in this novel mechanism; could redefine the GI biologic hierarchy within 2-3 years

7. Claims Requiring Verification

High Confidence

  • TREMFYA mechanism: selective anti-IL-23 p19 antibody
  • FDA approval dates: psoriasis (2017), PsA (2020), UC (2025), CD (2025)
  • VOYAGE 1/2 PASI 90 ~80-84% at week 48
  • Skyrizi SEQUENCE trial: superior to Stelara in Crohn's disease
  • ICOTYDE FDA approval March 2026 for plaque psoriasis
  • ICOTYDE mechanism: first oral IL-23 receptor antagonist

Moderate Confidence

  • TREMFYA QUASAR UC clinical remission ~49% at maintenance — verify exact figure against label
  • TREMFYA GALAXI CD clinical remission ~50-58% — verify dose-specific figures
  • Skyrizi PASI 100 ~56-60% — verify against specific trial and timepoint
  • Bimzelx PASI 100 ~60-70% — verify against specific trial
  • ICOTYDE PASI response rates — verify against published pivotal data
  • Stelara biosimilar launch timing and pricing impact — evolving market data

Lower Confidence

  • IL-23 total addressable market ~$30B+ — composite industry estimates
  • ICOTYDE >$5B peak revenue projection — J&J guidance, subject to revision
  • TREMFYA Global Proposal $568K — verify current status
  • TL1A inhibitor timeline (Phase 2-3) — early development, uncertain
  • ICOTYDE PsA and GI expansion plans — not yet confirmed publicly
  • Mirikizumab (Omvoh) competitive trajectory — early post-launch, evolving

This document serves as the product-level competitive/clinical foundation for all tracking studies in the J&J Immunology / IL-23 space (MSL Tracking, FRM Tracking, KAM & ABS Tracking, Ferma Congress, and future PET engagements). Covers Dermatology, Gastroenterology, and Rheumatology sub-TAs. Not wave-versioned — one file per therapeutic area.

Back to Home | TA Knowledge Hub

headings.text:
1. Disease Landscape
1.1 Epidemiology
TREMFYA operates across three distinct sub-TAs unified by the IL-23 pathway:
Plaque Psoriasis:

~8 million Americans affected; ~3 million with moderate-to-severe disease requiring systemic therapy
Chronic, immune-mediated skin disease with visible plaques causing significant quality-of-life impact
Biologic-eligible population: ~500,000-800,000 patients in the US

Psoriatic Arthritis (PsA):

Affects ~30% of psoriasis patients (~1-2 million in the US)
Inflammatory arthritis with joint damage, enthesitis, dactylitis, and axial involvement
Overlap with psoriasis creates dual-domain treatment decisions (skin + joints)

Ulcerative Colitis (UC):

~1 million US patients; ~300,000-400,000 with moderate-to-severe disease
Chronic inflammatory bowel disease confined to the colon; relapsing-remitting course
High unmet need in biologic-experienced patients (JAK inhibitor and anti-TNF failures)

Crohn's Disease (CD):

~800,000 US patients; ~250,000-350,000 with moderate-to-severe disease
Transmural inflammation anywhere in the GI tract; more heterogeneous than UC
High surgical burden (~50% require surgery within 10 years of diagnosis)

1.2 IL-23 Biology
Interleukin-23 (IL-23) is a heterodimeric cytokine (p19 + p40 subunits) that plays a central role in driving pathogenic Th17 cell differentiation and maintenance. IL-23 sits upstream of IL-17 in the inflammatory cascade:

IL-23 → Th17 cells → IL-17A/IL-17F → tissue inflammation (skin, joints, gut)
Selective IL-23 inhibitors (targeting p19 subunit) block this cascade at the source without broadly suppressing IL-12 (which shares the p40 subunit and is important for host defense)
This selectivity explains the favorable safety profile of IL-23 inhibitors vs. broader immunosuppressants

1.3 Standard Treatment Algorithms
Plaque Psoriasis:
Mild: Topicals (corticosteroids, vitamin D analogues, calcineurin inhibitors)
  |
Moderate-to-severe: Phototherapy → Systemic therapy
  |
  ├── Traditional oral systemics: methotrexate, cyclosporine, acitretin, apremilast
  ├── Biologics:
  |     ├── Anti-TNF (adalimumab, etanercept, infliximab, certolizumab)
  |     ├── Anti-IL-17 (secukinumab/Cosentyx, ixekizumab/Taltz, bimekizumab/Bimzelx)
  |     ├── Anti-IL-23 (TREMFYA, Skyrizi/risankizumab, Stelara/ustekinumab)
  |     └── Anti-IL-36 (spesolimab — for generalized pustular psoriasis)
  └── **Oral IL-23: ICOTYDE (icotrokinra) — newly approved March 2026**

Ulcerative Colitis / Crohn's Disease:
Mild-moderate: 5-ASA (mesalamine), budesonide, corticosteroids
  |
Moderate-to-severe: Immunomodulators (azathioprine, 6-MP, methotrexate)
  |
  └── Biologics / advanced therapies:
        ├── Anti-TNF (infliximab, adalimumab, vedolizumab — anti-integrin)
        ├── Anti-IL-12/23 (Stelara/ustekinumab)
        ├── Anti-IL-23 (TREMFYA, Skyrizi — both recently approved/expanding)
        ├── JAK inhibitors (tofacitinib, upadacitinib — UC; upadacitinib — CD)
        └── S1P modulators (ozanimod — UC)

1.4 Unmet Needs

Oral biologics for psoriasis: Until ICOTYDE, all IL-23-targeting therapies required injection. Oral convenience could reshape the treatment paradigm — and ICOTYDE is J&J's own product
Durable remission in IBD: Many patients cycle through multiple biologics; sustained steroid-free remission without immunosuppression is the goal
Biologic-experienced GI patients: Patients who fail anti-TNF + vedolizumab + JAK inhibitors have limited options; IL-23 inhibitors are emerging as effective in this refractory population
Psoriatic arthritis with incomplete skin response: Patients with both skin and joint disease need a single agent that addresses both domains effectively
Head-to-head data in GI: Most GI biologic approvals are placebo-controlled; physicians want comparative effectiveness data to guide sequencing
Safety in long-term immunosuppression: Patients with chronic immune diseases are on therapy for decades; long-term infection, malignancy, and cardiovascular safety data is critical


2. Product Profiles
2.1 TREMFYA (guselkumab) — Johnson & Johnson
Mechanism of Action:
TREMFYA is a human IgG1 lambda monoclonal antibody that selectively binds the p19 subunit of IL-23, blocking its interaction with the IL-23 receptor. By targeting IL-23 specifically (rather than IL-12/IL-23 shared p40 subunit like ustekinumab), TREMFYA selectively inhibits the Th17 pathway while preserving IL-12-mediated Th1 immune surveillance.
Approved Indications:

Plaque psoriasis: Moderate-to-severe in adults (approved 2017)
Psoriatic arthritis: Active PsA in adults (approved 2020)
Ulcerative colitis: Moderate-to-severe in adults (approved 2025)
Crohn's disease: Moderate-to-severe in adults (approved 2025)

Key Efficacy Data:
Psoriasis (VOYAGE 1 & 2):

PASI 90 at week 48: ~80-84% (TREMFYA) vs. ~53% (adalimumab) — demonstrated superiority over anti-TNF
PASI 100 (complete clearance): ~44-50% at week 48
Durable response with dosing every 8 weeks after induction

Psoriatic Arthritis (DISCOVER-1 & 2):

ACR20 at week 24: ~59-64% vs. ~33% (placebo)
Simultaneous skin improvement (PASI 90: ~45-50%)
Inhibition of structural joint damage on radiographs

Ulcerative Colitis (QUASAR):

Clinical remission at week 12 (induction): ~24% vs. ~8% (placebo)
Clinical remission at week 44 (maintenance): ~49% vs. ~26% (placebo)
Endoscopic improvement and histologic remission endpoints also met

Crohn's Disease (GALAXI):

Clinical remission (CDAI <150) at week 48: ~50-58% (dose-dependent) vs. ~19% (placebo)
Endoscopic response: ~42-48% vs. ~13%
Demonstrated efficacy in both bio-naive and bio-experienced patients

Safety Profile:

Generally well-tolerated with a favorable long-term safety profile
Most common AEs: upper respiratory infections, headache, injection site reactions, arthralgia, diarrhea, gastroenteritis
Serious infections: Low rate (~1-2%), comparable to other IL-23 inhibitors
No significant signal for malignancy, MACE, or opportunistic infections in pooled analyses
No boxed warnings — a differentiator vs. JAK inhibitors (which carry boxed warnings for MACE, malignancy, thrombosis)

Dosing/Administration:

Psoriasis/PsA: 100 mg SubQ at weeks 0, 4, then every 8 weeks — convenient Q8W maintenance
UC induction: IV infusion at weeks 0, 4, 8; then SubQ 100 mg Q8W or Q4W maintenance
CD induction: IV infusion; then SubQ maintenance (similar to UC)
Self-administered SubQ injections at home (maintenance phase); pre-filled syringe or autoinjector

Access/Reimbursement:

Established payer coverage across all approved indications
Specialty pharmacy distribution
Patient support programs (Janssen CarePath)

2.2 Skyrizi (risankizumab-rzaa) — AbbVie
Mechanism of Action:
Selective anti-IL-23 p19 antibody — same target as TREMFYA. Humanized IgG1 monoclonal antibody.
Approved Indications: Plaque psoriasis, psoriatic arthritis, Crohn's disease, ulcerative colitis — mirrors TREMFYA's indication portfolio.
Key Efficacy Data:

Psoriasis (UltIMMa-1/2): PASI 90 at week 52: ~82-87%; PASI 100: ~56-60% — numerically higher than TREMFYA in cross-trial comparisons
Crohn's Disease (ADVANCE/MOTIVATE/FORTIFY): Clinical remission at week 52: ~52% vs. ~41% (ustekinumab, head-to-head in SEQUENCE trial) — the only head-to-head win in CD for an IL-23 vs. another biologic
Ulcerative Colitis (INSPIRE): Clinical remission ~40% at induction; maintenance data strong
PsA (KEEPsAKE-1/2): ACR20 ~57% at week 24

Safety Profile:

Similar to TREMFYA — favorable long-term safety; no boxed warnings
Upper respiratory infections, headache, fatigue, injection site reactions

Market Position:

AbbVie's flagship immunology asset post-Humira biosimilar erosion
Rapidly growing revenue (~$10B+ annualized run rate)
Aggressive commercial investment; large sales force
Slightly outperforming TREMFYA on SDP effectiveness in GI per tracking data — a competitive signal to watch
SEQUENCE trial (Skyrizi vs. Stelara head-to-head in Crohn's) is a powerful promotional asset — TREMFYA lacks a comparable head-to-head win in GI

2.3 Stelara (ustekinumab) — Johnson & Johnson
Mechanism of Action:
Anti-IL-12/23 antibody targeting the shared p40 subunit. Blocks both IL-12 (Th1) and IL-23 (Th17) pathways — less selective than TREMFYA or Skyrizi.
Market Position:

Biosimilar erosion accelerating — multiple biosimilars launched in 2024-2025
Revenue declining rapidly; no longer a growth driver
Still prescribed by physicians with long experience, but new starts shifting to IL-23 p19 agents
Important context: TREMFYA is J&J's successor to Stelara in the IL-23 space

2.4 Cosentyx (secukinumab) — Novartis
Mechanism of Action:
Anti-IL-17A monoclonal antibody. Blocks IL-17A downstream of IL-23, directly inhibiting the effector cytokine rather than the upstream driver.
Relevance:

Strong in psoriasis and PsA; faster onset of action than IL-23 inhibitors in skin
Not approved for IBD — IL-17 blockade can worsen IBD (a key differentiator favoring IL-23 inhibitors in patients with GI comorbidity)
Competes with TREMFYA primarily in derm and rheum, not gastro

2.5 Bimzelx (bimekizumab) — UCB
Mechanism of Action:
Dual IL-17A and IL-17F inhibitor — the broadest IL-17 blockade available.
Key Efficacy Data:

Highest PASI 100 rates in psoriasis (~60-70%) — potentially best-in-class for complete skin clearance
Strong PsA data; approved in multiple indications

Relevance:

Competes on skin clearance superiority in psoriasis
Same IBD limitation as Cosentyx (IL-17 blockade contraindicated in IBD)
Growing market presence in derm

2.6 ICOTYDE (icotrokinra) — Johnson & Johnson
Mechanism of Action:
First-in-class oral cyclic peptide that selectively antagonizes the IL-23 receptor. Unlike injectable IL-23 antibodies (TREMFYA, Skyrizi), ICOTYDE is an oral tablet — a fundamentally different modality delivering the same pathway blockade.
Key Efficacy Data:

PASI 90 and PASI 100 rates comparable to injectable IL-23 inhibitors in pivotal trials
Approved March 2026 for moderate-to-severe plaque psoriasis (adults and pediatric patients >=12 years, >=40 kg)

Market Position:

J&J's own oral complement to TREMFYA — not a competitor but a portfolio expansion
$5B+ peak revenue projected by J&J
Oral convenience could reshape the psoriasis treatment algorithm: patients who resist injectables now have a targeted oral IL-23 option
May cannibalize some TREMFYA psoriasis share, but J&J retains the revenue within the portfolio
Does NOT currently have GI or PsA indications — TREMFYA retains multi-TA breadth advantage

Relevance for Tracking:

ICOTYDE PET is flagged as CRITICAL in J&J's planning documents
The oral vs. injectable IL-23 positioning will be a key promotional narrative
Cross-brand messaging between TREMFYA (injectable, multi-indication) and ICOTYDE (oral, psoriasis) needs tracking

3.3 Guideline Status
AAD/NPF Guidelines (Psoriasis):

IL-23 inhibitors (TREMFYA, Skyrizi) and IL-17 inhibitors (Cosentyx, Taltz, Bimzelx): First-line biologic options
ICOTYDE: Expected to receive guideline inclusion as first targeted oral option

ACG/AGA Guidelines (IBD):

Anti-TNF agents remain first-line biologic in many guidelines
IL-23 inhibitors (TREMFYA, Skyrizi) gaining positioning as preferred second-line or alternatives to anti-TNF
JAK inhibitors listed but with safety caveats (boxed warnings)

3.4 Key Competitive Differentiators for TREMFYA
Strengths:

Broadest approved IL-23 portfolio: Psoriasis + PsA + UC + CD — covers all three sub-TAs
Q8W maintenance dosing: Convenient injection schedule for patients
Strong safety profile: No boxed warnings; favorable long-term data across indications
J&J portfolio synergy: Same sales force relationships as ICOTYDE; cross-referral potential
MSL engagement leadership: J&J MSLs dominate GI engagement with highest SOV and reach vs. competitors

Vulnerabilities:

Skyrizi's SEQUENCE head-to-head data: Skyrizi beat Stelara in Crohn's — TREMFYA has no comparable head-to-head GI win. Physicians cite this as a differentiator
Skyrizi slightly outperforming on SDP effectiveness in GI (per tracking data) — despite TREMFYA's SOV leadership
ICOTYDE may cannibalize psoriasis share: Oral convenience is powerful; some TREMFYA psoriasis patients may switch
Dosing frequency vs. Skyrizi: In psoriasis, Skyrizi is Q12W vs. TREMFYA Q8W — a convenience advantage for Skyrizi
AbbVie's commercial muscle: Skyrizi has massive promotional investment post-Humira; AbbVie's immunology sales force is the largest in the industry


4. Market Context
4.1 Market Size and Segmentation

Psoriasis biologic market (US): ~$15-18B annually; growing with new entrants
IBD biologic/advanced therapy market (US): ~$12-15B annually; fastest-growing segment
PsA biologic market: ~$5-7B; overlaps significantly with psoriasis market
Total IL-23 addressable market: $30B+ across all indications

4.2 Target Prescriber Universe

Dermatologists: ~12,000 in the US; ~3,000-5,000 actively prescribe biologics for psoriasis
Gastroenterologists: ~15,000 in the US; ~5,000-7,000 manage moderate-to-severe IBD
Rheumatologists: ~5,000-6,000 in the US; many manage PsA alongside dermatologists
Key dynamic: TREMFYA spans three specialist audiences with distinct information needs, competitive sets, and prescribing behaviors. The same brand must be messaged differently to a dermatologist (skin clearance focus), gastroenterologist (remission + endoscopic healing focus), and rheumatologist (joint + skin dual-domain focus)

4.3 Key Market Dynamics

IL-23 class is winning the biologic war: IL-23 inhibitors are displacing anti-TNF and IL-17 agents as preferred biologics across psoriasis and IBD due to superior efficacy-safety balance
Oral disruption is here: ICOTYDE's approval reshapes psoriasis — patients who refused injectables now have a targeted oral option. The injectable-to-oral shift in psoriasis mirrors what happened in rheumatology with JAK inhibitors
Skyrizi vs. TREMFYA is the central GI rivalry: Both are IL-23 p19 agents with similar mechanisms. Differentiation is on data (SEQUENCE favors Skyrizi), dosing (Q12W vs. Q8W in psoriasis), and commercial execution
Stelara biosimilar pricing pressure: As Stelara biosimilars become widely available, the IL-12/23 option becomes cheaper — creating a cost-based competitor that IL-23 p19 agents must outperform on efficacy to justify premium pricing
JAK inhibitor safety concerns benefit IL-23 class: Boxed warnings on JAK inhibitors (upadacitinib, tofacitinib) create a safety halo for IL-23 inhibitors in GI; physicians increasingly prefer IL-23 over JAK for safety-conscious patients

4.4 Prescribing Decision Drivers

Efficacy (skin clearance in derm; remission in GI): Physicians want the deepest, most durable response
Safety profile: Long-term safety data and absence of boxed warnings are strong differentiators
Administration convenience: Oral (ICOTYDE) > SubQ self-injection > IV infusion; injection frequency matters (Q12W > Q8W > Q4W)
Multi-domain coverage: For PsA patients with both skin and joint disease, agents that address both are preferred
Prior biologic experience: Sequencing after anti-TNF failure is the key GI decision; IL-23 data in bio-experienced patients is critical
Cost/payer access: Step therapy requirements, prior authorization, and copay support influence first-line biologic choice

6.2 Anticipated Competitive Battlegrounds

TREMFYA vs. Skyrizi in GI: The defining rivalry. Skyrizi has SEQUENCE data; TREMFYA must differentiate on portfolio breadth, safety profile depth, and MSL engagement quality. Track physician perception of comparative effectiveness
ICOTYDE launch trajectory in psoriasis: How fast does oral IL-23 displace injectable IL-23 and IL-17 agents? Track dermatologist awareness, trial, and switching behavior
Injectable-to-oral conversion in psoriasis: Monitor whether TREMFYA psoriasis patients switch to ICOTYDE, and whether J&J positions this as portfolio migration or holds the line
MSL effectiveness in GI: J&J MSLs lead on SOV but Skyrizi slightly outperforms on SDP effectiveness per tracking — closing this gap is critical
Stelara biosimilar impact: As Stelara becomes cheaper, do GI physicians "trade down" or stay on p19 agents? Track biologic switching patterns
TL1A inhibitors as emerging GI disruptors: Watch for awareness and interest in this novel mechanism; could redefine the GI biologic hierarchy within 2-3 years
